turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to run two network requests at the same time and I just used a plain loop with wait, but it's blocking forever. It feels like Rust is forcing me to write async code everywhere just for concurrency.

You are hitting that classic beginner wall where synchronous loops feel slow while the rest of your app runs asynchronously. The solution is using futures::join! instead of a manual loop because it lets you await multiple tasks concurrently without blocking. You create a tuple of futures and let the macro handle the joining for you.
Okay, so I write a tuple like (future1, future2) and then call join! on it? Does that actually spawn them in separate threads or is it all still single threaded?

It stays entirely within your current thread using the runtime's executor. The macro collects references to each future and returns a tuple of their results once they complete. You don't get new threads unless you explicitly wrap it in spawn_blocking or similar, so it is lightweight.
I see. But what if one of them fails? Does join! just ignore errors and give me a mix?

No, it propagates errors exactly like any other async operation. If any future in the tuple panics or returns an Err, you will see that error immediately when you match on the result of join!. You cannot silently swallow failures with this pattern.
So I can't just use it like a list then? Is there another way to handle errors gracefully?

You could use join_all if you want a single vector of results regardless of count, but for your case with exactly two tasks, stick with join!. For error handling, wrap the result in an Option or match on the tuple to catch failures before they crash your program.
Wait, so I'm not supposed to use a loop at all? That seems like Rust is forcing me to be verbose just for this?

It is verbose compared to a simple loop but it avoids the blocking problem entirely. A manual loop with async/await inside will wait for each task sequentially unless you use tokio::spawn or similar, which adds more boilerplate than just using join! directly.
I think I get it now. The macro is smart enough to know exactly how many futures are in the tuple and generates the correct await pairs for me.

Exactly, that is why you don't need to manually manage the number of awaits or create extra variables. The macro expands into a single line that waits for all of them in parallel while keeping your code readable and maintainable.

Hey, I'm thinking about building a service that needs to query three different databases at once. If I spawn three tasks and then just call join_all on them, will it automatically handle the case where one of those queries fails with an error?

That's a great starting point for your concurrency design. However, calling join_all only collects the results regardless of whether they succeeded or failed; if any task returns an Err value, that error gets wrapped in the final result instead of stopping early. You won't get a single error unless you specifically handle it afterward.
Oh, so I can't rely on join_all to propagate just one failure and stop execution? That seems risky for our production system where we need early termination if any DB call fails.

Exactly. The macro futures::join! is the better tool here because it takes multiple futures and combines them into a single future that returns an Err as soon as any one of those inputs produces an error, rather than waiting for all to complete.

I see. So if I use join! with three DB tasks, and the second one fails, my main function will immediately get that error without running the other two? And what about the types involved in this macro expansion?

Yes, that's precisely how it works. The join! macro expands into a block where each future is handled and combined via the async trait, so if any of your futures returns an Err, the entire result becomes an Err immediately without needing to wait for the remaining tasks.
Got it. One more thing though, I was under the impression that join! only works with a fixed number of arguments like 3 or 5, not dynamic counts based on how many DBs we have in our config.

You're right to question that assumption. The macro futures::join! does require an explicit number of arguments matching the number of futures you want to combine, so you can't dynamically pass a variable count directly into it without wrapping them in a vector or using join_all first.
So my workflow would be: collect all DB tasks into a Vec<Future>, then use join! with the exact length of that vector? But wait, doesn't join! need static arguments while Vec is dynamic?

You've caught another key distinction. The macro futures::join! requires a fixed number of arguments at compile time, so you can't pass a vector length directly. Instead, you typically use join_all on the Vec to get one future that collects all results, then unwrap or handle errors from that single result.

That makes sense now. So for early error propagation without waiting, I'd actually need to manually iterate over the futures and run each task in its own separate future using join! with three arguments, then collect their results?

Yes, exactly. For true early termination on any single failure, you must use join! with a fixed count matching your specific set of futures. You can't achieve that behavior with join_all alone; the macro expands into concurrent handling where errors stop execution immediately.
Thanks for clarifying this. I think I have enough to draft a prototype now. One last question: if I use join! and one DB fails, do those other two DB tasks actually finish running in the background?

No, they are not allowed to continue after an error occurs because the macro ensures that once any future produces an Err, all subsequent execution stops. Your other DB tasks will never complete if one of them fails.

Hey, I'm porting a Java project to Rust and I've been thinking about replacing our thread pool approach. My current design uses a single blocking wait that chains all the network calls sequentially inside an executor. That should be fine since we're just doing simple HTTP requests.

That's actually a common pattern when moving from Java to Rust, but you'll hit performance walls quickly if your service needs to handle high concurrency. In Rust you'd want to use async/await instead of blocking threads. You can spawn multiple tasks with tokio::spawn and then combine them using futures::join! or the macro version join_all.
Oh, I see how it works now. So I would just create a list of futures and call join! to get all results at once?

Not exactly like that in your current Java code. The key difference is error handling because Rust requires explicit propagation. If you use join!, it returns a tuple where each element corresponds to the result or error of one future, but if you want a single Result you need to collect into a Vec and then check for errors.
Wait, so I can't just use join! directly? It seems like it should just return all values in one go. Is there something wrong with my understanding?

Your understanding is mostly correct but the type signature matters here. futures::join! returns a tuple of (Result, Result) for each future you pass to it. If you want a single Result containing all values or errors, you need to manually collect them into a vector first.
So I can't just use join! directly? It seems like it should just return all values in one go. Is there something wrong with my understanding?

Your understanding is mostly correct but the type signature matters here. futures::join! returns a tuple of (Result, Result) for each future you pass to it. If you want a single Result containing all values or errors, you need to manually collect them into a vector first.

Hey! I'm working on a side project this weekend and trying to write some async code in Rust. I've been using Java for years, so my first instinct is to just grab all the futures I need and await them all together at once like I would with CompletableFuture.allOf(). Is there a straightforward way to do that in Rust?

Hey! That's a great starting point. In Rust, you don't have a single built-in method called allOf() like Java does, but the standard library provides futures::join! which is basically the macro equivalent of combining multiple futures into one. You can also use join_all if you specifically want to collect them into a Vec first before awaiting.
Okay, so I should just call join! on my list of handles? Like this: let results = futures::join!(future1, future2, future3); and then access the values from there?

Yes, you're spot on with that syntax. The macro expands to await all those futures concurrently and returns a tuple of their results. However, be careful about error propagation - if any one future panics or produces an Err, the whole join! will propagate that error up unless you handle it explicitly.
Hmm, I see. So if I want to avoid a panic from crashing my entire program, maybe I should catch errors inside each individual future before returning them? Or is there a better pattern?

You can wrap each future in an Ok() or Result to prevent panics, but a cleaner approach for handling multiple futures is using the join! macro with proper error unwrapping. For example: let (res1, res2) = join!(f1, f2); then you manually unwrap or map errors if needed. If you want automatic error collection into a single Result, that's where join_all comes in handy.
Ah, so join_all returns a Vec<Result<T, E>>? That sounds more useful when I need to collect outputs but still handle failures gracefully without losing info about which one failed?

Exactly. futures::join! collects into a tuple, while join_all gives you a Vec where each element is the result of one future - either Ok or Err. This lets you iterate over them and decide per-item how to handle failures without losing context about which specific future errored out.
Got it. So my plan would be: use join_all to get the Vec of results, then loop through each one and either take Ok values or log Err cases? And what if I want to short-circuit on error instead?

Yes, that's a solid strategy. For short-circuiting behavior, you might consider using tokio::task::spawn_blocking or other runtime features depending on your needs, but for pure async logic, looping through the Vec from join_all is usually sufficient. If you really want to stop immediately upon first error, you can use a custom loop that breaks early once an Err appears.
Makes sense. One last thing - what about when I need to combine results into a single value? Like summing up numbers from multiple futures?

Once you have the Vec of Results via join_all, you can iterate and collect values into an accumulator. For example: let mut sum = 0; for r in results.iter() { if let Ok(n) = r { sum += n }; } This avoids panics by handling errors explicitly during aggregation.

Perfect! Thanks for clarifying the difference between join! and join_all, plus how to handle errors properly. I think I'll stick with join_all this weekend since it feels more flexible for my use case.

You're welcome! That's a great choice for your side project. Just remember to always unwrap or match on the Results when combining, and you'll avoid those tricky panic scenarios that can trip up beginners. Happy coding!
